title: How does the Java 9 module system (Project Jigsaw) improve encapsulation and modularity?

answers.junior: The Java 9 module system, introduced in Project Jigsaw, enforces strong encapsulation by making all internal packages hidden by default, unlike the previous package-private visibility which was accessible through reflection and classpath manipulation. Modules explicitly declare their exported public API through module-info.java files, for example a module can specify module myapp { exports com.example.api; } to expose only the api package while keeping internal implementation packages entirely inaccessible.
answers.middle: This goes beyond traditional encapsulation because external code cannot access non-exported packages even through reflection or classpath tricks, providing true compile-time and runtime enforced boundaries. The module system improves modularity by enabling explicit dependency declaration, allowing developers to see exactly which modules depend on which others through requires statements, making dependency graphs transparent and preventing accidental circular dependencies. Modules can use requires transitive to establish clear API boundaries, where a module can require another module and automatically expose it to modules that depend on it, creating cleaner dependency chains.
answers.senior: The platform itself was modularized into 94 platform modules, dramatically reducing the JDK footprint for embedded and lightweight deployments where you only include needed modules rather than shipping the entire monolithic JDK. Service providers are a critical feature where modules can expose implementations of interfaces without exposing the interface itself, for instance module myservice { provides com.example.Service with com.example.ServiceImpl; } enables loose coupling and plugin-like architectures. A significant production benefit is improved security and maintenance, as internal APIs are now truly hidden, forcing framework and library developers to use only stable public APIs rather than relying on fragile implementation details like sun.misc.Unsafe.
answers.tricky: While adoption has been gradual in enterprise environments due to migration challenges with existing libraries and classpath dependencies, understanding modules is essential for building scalable, maintainable systems and transitioning away from the catch-all classpath model.
